exercises.title: The six core gateway features
exercises.prompt: An AI gateway sits between apps and providers, offering: provider routing, fallback, retries, rate limiting, secret references, observability, guardrails. Build a minimal gateway class implementing routing + fallback + retries, where a call to a primary provider that fails (e.g. 429) automatically falls back to a secondary, with bounded retries. Show a request surviving a primary outage via fallback.
exercises.hint: Gateway.call(request): try primary with up to N retries on transient errors; if still failing, fall back to secondary provider. Simulate primary returning 429, secondary succeeding. Show the request succeeding via fallback rather than failing.

tests.desc: a failing primary falls back to the secondary
both providers down returns a failure

exercises.title: Select a gateway by RPS and requirements
exercises.prompt: Gateways differ sharply: LiteLLM (MIT, 100+ providers, breaks ~2000 RPS), Portkey (control-plane, guardrails/PII, Apache 2.0, ~20-40ms overhead), Kong AI Gateway (fast, enterprise-fit if on Kong), Bifrost (auto retries + fallback). Build `pick_gateway(requirements)` keyed on RPS, language, guardrail needs, and existing infra, and recommend with reasoning. Show a prototype picking LiteLLM and a high-RPS enterprise picking Kong.
exercises.hint: LiteLLM: <500 RPS, Python, dev/prototyping. Portkey: need guardrails/PII/audit, mid scale. Kong: high RPS + already on Kong. Bifrost: need robust retries/fallback. Decide on RPS + needs + infra. Show LiteLLM for prototype, Kong for high-RPS enterprise.
exercises.solution:
Gateways are not interchangeable — they have sharply different scale ceilings and feature focuses. Picking the wrong one means either over-engineering a prototype or hitting a throughput wall in production.

```python
def pick_gateway(req):
    if req['rps'] > 2000:
        if req['on_kong']:
            return 'Kong AI Gateway', 'high RPS + already on Kong (228% faster than Portkey in their bench)'
        return 'Portkey or Kong', 'LiteLLM breaks down ~2000 RPS; need a faster control plane'
    if req['need_guardrails']:
        return 'Portkey', 'control-plane: guardrails, PII redaction, jailbreak detection, audit'
    if req['rps'] < 500 and req['python']:
        return 'LiteLLM', 'MIT OSS, 100+ providers, OpenAI-compatible; ideal for Python dev/prototyping'
    return 'Bifrost', 'robust auto-retries + fallback'

profiles = {
    'python prototype': {'rps': 100, 'python': True, 'need_guardrails': False, 'on_kong': False},
    'high-RPS enterprise on Kong': {'rps': 5000, 'python': False, 'need_guardrails': True, 'on_kong': True},
}
for name, p in profiles.items():
    gw, why = pick_gateway(p)
    print(f"{name:>28} -> {gw}\n{'':>32}{why}")
```

Output:

```
            python prototype -> LiteLLM
                                MIT OSS, 100+ providers, OpenAI-compatible; ideal for Python dev/prototyping
high-RPS enterprise on Kong -> Kong AI Gateway
                                high RPS + already on Kong (228% faster than Portkey in their bench)
```

**The scale ceiling is the first filter.** LiteLLM is excellent for Python prototyping (MIT, 100+ providers, OpenAI-compatible) but published benchmarks show it breaking down around ~2000 RPS (8 GB memory, cascading failures). Deploy it at high RPS and you get an outage from your *resilience layer* — the opposite of its purpose. So the first question is RPS: under ~500 RPS, LiteLLM is great; above ~2000, you need a faster control plane (Kong, Portkey). The prototype picks LiteLLM; the 5000-RPS enterprise picks Kong (especially since they are already on Kong, and Kong's own benchmark claims 228% faster than Portkey, 859% faster than LiteLLM on the same hardware).

**Match features to needs, infra to infra.** Beyond RPS: Portkey is control-plane-positioned (guardrails, PII redaction, jailbreak detection, audit trails — went Apache 2.0 in March 2026), so pick it when you need those governance features; Kong fits if you are already on Kong Gateway; Bifrost focuses on robust retries/fallback. Data residency drives the self-host decision — Portkey and Kong sit in the middle with OSS + optional managed, while Cloudflare/Vercel gateways are managed zero-ops with basic retry. As with every selection lesson, the choice follows requirements (RPS, language, guardrails, existing infra), not reputation. The final exercise covers the rate-limiting feature in depth.

tests.desc: high RPS on Kong picks the Kong gateway
a low-RPS Python shop picks LiteLLM

exercises.title: Multi-tenant rate limiting and budget enforcement
exercises.prompt: A gateway enforces rate limits and budgets per tenant to prevent one tenant from starving others or running up the bill. Build a gateway rate limiter with per-tenant RPS limits (token bucket) and a per-tenant daily spend cap, returning clear 429 + retry-after when a tenant exceeds RPS and pausing a tenant that hits its spend cap. Show one noisy tenant being throttled while others are unaffected.
exercises.hint: Per tenant: token bucket (refills at rate, capacity = burst). On request: if tokens available, allow + spend; else 429 with retry-after. Also track daily spend; if over cap, pause tenant. Show a noisy tenant hitting 429 / spend cap while a quiet tenant flows normally. Isolation between tenants.
exercises.solution:
A shared gateway must isolate tenants: one tenant's runaway traffic or spend cannot be allowed to degrade others or blow the bill. Per-tenant rate limits and budget caps provide that isolation.

```python
import time

class MultiTenantLimiter:
    def __init__(self, rps_limit, daily_cap):
        self.rps_limit = rps_limit
        self.daily_cap = daily_cap
        self.tokens = {}        # tenant -> available request tokens (bucket)
        self.spend = {}         # tenant -> daily spend

    def request(self, tenant, cost):
        self.tokens.setdefault(tenant, self.rps_limit)
        self.spend.setdefault(tenant, 0.0)
        # budget cap: pause tenant over its daily spend
        if self.spend[tenant] + cost > self.daily_cap:
            return f"PAUSED: {tenant} hit daily spend cap (${self.daily_cap})"
        # rate limit: token bucket
        if self.tokens[tenant] <= 0:
            return f"429 {tenant}: rate limit, retry-after 1s"
        self.tokens[tenant] -= 1
        self.spend[tenant] += cost
        return f"OK {tenant} (spend ${self.spend[tenant]:.0f})"

lim = MultiTenantLimiter(rps_limit=3, daily_cap=100)
# noisy tenant bursts past its RPS; quiet tenant unaffected
for _ in range(5):
    print(lim.request('noisy', cost=10))
print(lim.request('quiet', cost=10))    # quiet tenant flows normally
```

Output:

```
OK noisy (spend $10)
OK noisy (spend $20)
OK noisy (spend $30)
429 noisy: rate limit, retry-after 1s
429 noisy: rate limit, retry-after 1s
OK quiet (spend $10)
```

**Tenant isolation is the point.** The noisy tenant bursts past its RPS limit (3 requests) and gets clean 429s with retry-after — but the quiet tenant is *completely unaffected* (it flows normally on its own bucket). Without per-tenant limits, the noisy tenant's burst would consume shared capacity and degrade everyone (the noisy-neighbor problem). The token bucket gives each tenant its own allowance with a clear, actionable error (429 + retry-after tells the client exactly when to retry), and the daily spend cap prevents any single tenant from running up an unbounded bill.

**Why the gateway is the right place for this.** Rate limiting and budgets are cross-cutting concerns that must apply uniformly across all apps and providers — exactly what the gateway centralizes. Enforcing them at the gateway means you cannot bypass them by calling a provider directly, and you get consistent 429 semantics and spend tracking regardless of which backend served the request. This connects to the FinOps lesson (Lesson 27): the gateway is where per-tenant attribution and the enforcement ladder (rate limits → spend caps → kill switches) live, because it sees every request with its tenant tag.

**The gateway synthesis.** A gateway provides resilience (routing, retries, fallback — Exercise 1) so requests survive provider outages, is selected by RPS and requirements (Exercise 2) so the resilience layer does not itself become the bottleneck, and enforces per-tenant rate limits and budgets (this exercise) so one tenant cannot harm others or the bill. It is the single chokepoint where you implement resilience, governance, and cost control *once* for all apps — which is why it is the center of the production LLM stack and the natural home for secrets (Lesson 25), observability (Lesson 13), and FinOps enforcement (Lesson 27). Choose it carefully: it is load-bearing, so its scale ceiling and feature set must match your production reality, not your prototype's.

tests.desc: a request within limits is OK
the rate limit kicks in after the bucket empties
the daily budget cap pauses a tenant
tenants are isolated from each other
